def: The basic unit of life — all living things are made of cells
A cell with NO nucleus and NO membrane-bound organelles. Example: bacteria.
A cell WITH a nucleus and WITH membrane-bound organelles. Example: plant and animal cells.
A small structure inside a cell that has a specific job — means "little organ"
The dense center — in a cell, the control center holding DNA; in an atom, the core containing protons and neutrons
The molecule that carries genetic instructions for life — shaped like a twisted ladder (double helix)
DNA in its spread-out, tangled form — like a messy pile of yarn
DNA coiled up tightly into a compact X or rod shape — happens when a cell is ready to divide
A tiny machine that builds proteins — made in the nucleolus
The flexible outer boundary of ALL cells — controls what enters and leaves
The jelly-like fluid filling the cell — everything between the membrane and the nucleus
The network of organelles that builds, processes, and ships proteins — includes ER, Golgi, and vesicles
Network of folded membranes covered in ribosomes — folds and transports proteins (the "highway")
Stacked membranes that modify, package, and sort proteins for delivery — the "packaging center"
A small membrane bubble that carries materials between organelles
Organelle that breaks down waste, damaged parts, and foreign invaders — the "garbage collector"
Organelle that converts glucose into ATP energy — the "engine" or "powerhouse" of the cell
Organelle that captures sunlight to make glucose through photosynthesis — only in plant cells
The energy molecule cells use to do work — like a rechargeable battery
The green pigment inside chloroplasts that absorbs sunlight energy
A thick, rigid outer layer made of cellulose — gives plant cells their square shape
One very large water storage compartment in plant cells — keeps the plant firm when full
The pressure of water inside the central vacuole pushing against the cell wall — keeps plants firm
The material that makes plant cell walls rigid — gives plants structural support
Small structures in animal cells that help organize cell division
The process by which plants use sunlight to convert CO₂ and water into glucose and oxygen
The process by which cells break down glucose to release ATP energy — happens in mitochondria
A small, dense area inside the nucleus where ribosomes are made
The double membrane surrounding the nucleus — has pores that let messages in and out
A lipid with a hydrophilic phosphate head and two hydrophobic fatty acid tails — the building block of cell membranes
Two layers of phospholipids arranged tail-to-tail — the basic structure of all cell membranes
Water-loving — dissolves in or is attracted to water (polar or charged molecules)
Water-fearing — does NOT dissolve in water (nonpolar molecules like oils and fats)
Having both a water-loving (hydrophilic) part and a water-fearing (hydrophobic) part in the same molecule
Allows some materials through but not others — 'semi' means partial
The scientific model of the cell membrane — 'fluid' because parts move freely, 'mosaic' because it has many different pieces
A molecule that sits between phospholipids and stabilizes the membrane — keeps it not too stiff and not too loose
A protein permanently embedded in the membrane — often goes all the way through both layers
A protein that sits on the surface of the membrane — only temporarily attached, does not go through the bilayer
A protein with carbohydrate (sugar) chains attached — acts as an ID tag on the cell surface
A lipid with carbohydrate (sugar) chains attached — part of the cell's identity coating on the outer surface
The sugar-coated outer surface of a cell made of glycoproteins and glycolipids — like an ID card that the immune system reads
A membrane protein that forms a tunnel (pore) for specific ions or small molecules to pass through
A membrane protein that grabs a molecule, changes shape, and releases it on the other side of the membrane
A membrane protein that receives chemical signals from outside the cell and triggers a response inside
A process where atoms rearrange — bonds break and new bonds form, turning reactants into products
A starting substance in a chemical reaction — what you begin with
A new substance formed at the end of a chemical reaction
The minimum energy needed to start a chemical reaction — like pushing a boulder up a hill
A substance that speeds up a chemical reaction by lowering activation energy, without being used up
A protein that acts as a biological catalyst — speeds up reactions by lowering activation energy
The specific pocket or groove on an enzyme where the substrate fits and the reaction happens
The molecule that fits into an enzyme's active site and gets changed by the reaction
When a protein loses its 3D shape and stops working — like bending a key so it no longer fits
The temperature at which an enzyme works best — for human enzymes, around 37°C
The pH at which an enzyme works best — different enzymes have different optimal pH values
Weak bonds that hold a protein's 3D shape together — break during denaturation
A model where the enzyme and substrate fit together perfectly, like a key into a lock
A model where the enzyme changes shape slightly to grip the substrate tighter — like a glove tightening
A process where atoms rearrange — bonds break and new bonds form, turning reactants into products
A starting substance in a chemical reaction — what you begin with
A new substance formed at the end of a chemical reaction
The minimum energy needed to start a chemical reaction — like pushing a boulder up a hill
A substance that speeds up a chemical reaction by lowering activation energy, without being used up
The random bouncing movement of tiny molecules — like a room full of bouncy balls
A protein that acts as a biological catalyst — speeds up reactions by lowering activation energy
The specific pocket or groove on an enzyme where the substrate fits and the reaction happens
The molecule that fits into an enzyme's active site and gets changed by the reaction
A model where the enzyme and substrate fit together perfectly, like a key into a lock
A model where the enzyme changes shape slightly to grip the substrate tighter — like a glove tightening
When a protein loses its 3D shape and stops working — like bending a key so it no longer fits the lock
The temperature at which an enzyme works best — for human enzymes, around 37°C
The pH at which an enzyme works best — different enzymes have different optimal pH values
Weak bonds that hold a protein's 3D shape together — they break when conditions change too much
A very large molecule made of smaller repeating units (monomers) — the four types are carbohydrates, lipids, proteins, and nucleic acids
A small molecule that bonds with others to form a polymer — the building block
A large molecule made of many monomers bonded together in a chain
A simple sugar — the building block (monomer) of carbohydrates. Examples: glucose, fructose
A long chain of many sugar monomers bonded together — examples: starch, glycogen, cellulose
A fat molecule made of one glycerol and three fatty acid chains — stores long-term energy
The building block (monomer) of proteins — there are 20 types, each with a unique R group (side chain)
The side chain of an amino acid that makes it unique — determines if it is polar, nonpolar, acidic, or basic
The building block (monomer) of DNA and RNA — made of a phosphate group, a sugar, and a nitrogenous base
The twisted ladder shape of DNA — two sugar-phosphate backbones connected by base pairs
The rule that A always pairs with T (or U in RNA) and G always pairs with C
The part of a nucleotide that carries the genetic 'letter' — A, T, G, C in DNA or A, U, G, C in RNA
Movement of molecules from high to low concentration — no energy needed (down the gradient)
Movement of molecules from low to high concentration — requires ATP energy (against the gradient)
The diffusion of water across a membrane — water moves toward the side with more dissolved particles (solute)
The process where the cell membrane wraps around material to bring it INTO the cell
The process where a vesicle fuses with the cell membrane to push material OUT of the cell
A protein that helps other proteins fold correctly by providing a protected environment
A fat with NO double bonds between carbons — straight chains pack tightly, so it is solid at room temperature
A fat with one or more double bonds — kinked chains cannot pack tightly, so it is liquid at room temperature
A bond where atoms share electrons — the strongest type of bond within a molecule
A molecule with a slightly positive end and a slightly negative end because electrons are shared unequally — like a tiny magnet
The attraction between molecules of the SAME substance — water sticking to water due to hydrogen bonds
The attraction between molecules of DIFFERENT substances — water sticking to glass, paper, or plant tubes
A 'skin' on the water's surface caused by extra hydrogen bonds between surface molecules — lets insects walk on water
The ability of water to move upward through narrow tubes against gravity — uses adhesion and cohesion together
The amount of energy needed to raise the temperature of a substance — water's is very high because energy breaks hydrogen bonds first
When water evaporates it takes heat energy with it and cools what's left behind — this is how sweating cools your body
The tissue in plants that transports water and minerals upward from roots to leaves — like tiny straws inside the stem
Maintaining stable internal conditions — your body constantly works to keep temperature, pH, and water balance in a narrow range
The smallest unit of an element — made of protons, neutrons, and electrons
A particle smaller than an atom — protons, neutrons, and electrons
A subatomic particle with a positive (+1) charge and mass of about 1 amu — found in the nucleus
A subatomic particle with no charge (neutral) and mass of about 1 amu — found in the nucleus
A subatomic particle with a negative (−1) charge and almost no mass — found in the electron cloud
The region around the nucleus where electrons move rapidly — mostly empty space
The number of protons in an atom — this number defines which element the atom is
The total number of protons + neutrons in the nucleus — a whole number for a specific atom
Atoms of the SAME element (same protons) with DIFFERENT numbers of neutrons — same element, different mass
The weighted average mass of all naturally occurring isotopes of an element — shown as a decimal on the periodic table
A region around the nucleus where electrons are found — also called a shell
An electron in the outermost energy level — determines bonding and chemical properties
An electron in an inner energy level — held tightly near the nucleus and does NOT participate in bonding
